name: Amy Beliveau
background: Six-year Winchester resident with more than a decade of experience in development, design, negotiation, and real estate. She said she has served on Town Meeting, the EFPBC, and the State of the Town Committee.
summary: Amy Beliveau won a Planning Board seat in 2026, running as a challenger with real estate and development experience. In campaign materials and a candidate forum, she emphasized balanced growth, data-driven decision-making, collaboration, and adding housing while respecting Winchester’s history.
locationNote: Winchester resident; Town Meeting member for precinct seven.
priorRecordLabel: Town Meeting and town committee service
priorRecordSummary: Beliveau cited service on Town Meeting, the EFPBC, and the State of the Town Committee, but the provided sources do not show a prior Planning Board voting record.

stanceCards.label: No school facilities position in provided sources
stanceCards.detail: The provided sources focus on planning, housing, zoning, and growth, not school facilities.

stanceCards.label: Supports expanding housing options
stanceCards.detail: At the forum, Beliveau said larger buildings can still offer housing opportunities and urged the town to consider interior conversions of large homes in single-family neighborhoods.

stanceCards.label: Emphasizes financial analysis in planning decisions
stanceCards.detail: She said she understands project financials and what levers the town can use to create value and achieve its objectives, and described herself as a data-driven decision maker.

evidence.title: Candidate profile outlines development and town-government background
evidence.summary: Beliveau said she would bring over a decade of development, design, and negotiation experience, plus service on Town Meeting, the EFPBC, and the State of the Town Committee. She emphasized real estate knowledge, project finance, and data-driven collaboration.

evidence.title: Forum comments on housing growth and preservation
evidence.summary: At the Planning Board forum, Beliveau said she brings real estate industry knowledge the board lacks, argued historic character is not limited to small buildings, and said larger buildings can still provide housing opportunities.

evidence.title: Beliveau backs broader housing strategies
evidence.summary: She urged Winchester to look beyond transit-oriented corridors under the MBTA Communities Act and consider interior conversions of large homes in single-family neighborhoods.

evidence.title: Election-night coverage says Beliveau unseated the incumbent
evidence.summary: Winchester News reported that Beliveau unseated incumbent Nicholas Rossettos to claim one of two open Planning Board seats.

evidence.title: Official results show Beliveau elected to Planning Board
evidence.summary: Official town results list Planning Board votes with Keri Layton at 2,331, Amy S. Beliveau at 2,042, and Nicholas J. Rossettos at 1,864 for two seats, placing Beliveau second and in a winning position.

name: Heather von Mering
background: Mother of five in Winchester Public Schools; works in construction and asset planning as a vice president at Von Salmi & Associates. Served 16 years in Winchester town government, including the Historical Commission, Planning Board, Capital Planning Committee, Town Moderator, and Town Meeting.
summary: Von Mering ran for School Committee in 2026 emphasizing parent experience, literacy reform, special education advocacy, facilities planning, and municipal finance experience. She argued the district should focus on closing literacy and math gaps and supported funding the school budget as requested in 2025. She lost the 2026 election to John Bellaire.
locationNote: Winchester resident; moved to Winchester in 2009.
priorRecordLabel: Town government and Town Meeting record
priorRecordSummary: Von Mering had extensive town-government service and took a visible role in 2025 Town Meeting school budget debate, but the sources do not show a prior School Committee voting record.

stanceCards.label: Backs literacy reform and early identification
stanceCards.detail: She described herself as a long-time literacy reform advocate, supported the district’s new literacy curriculum, and emphasized early identification of dyslexia and stronger foundational instruction and Tier 2 supports.

stanceCards.label: Supported full requested school budget in 2025
stanceCards.detail: At Town Meeting, she opposed using Circuit Breaker funds for the FY26 budget and urged funding the School Committee’s requested budget, with free cash used to offset the deficit.

stanceCards.label: Brings facilities planning background
stanceCards.detail: She said her construction and asset-planning work informs her approach to facilities governance, long-term capital planning, budgeting, and stewardship of school buildings.

stanceCards.label: Emphasizes finance and transparent process
stanceCards.detail: She cited long service on the town-government side, including capital planning and town moderator roles, and said that experience shaped her views on municipal finance, public process, transparency, and disciplined goal setting.

stanceCards.label: Would defer to expertise on tech policy
stanceCards.detail: On artificial intelligence and school technology policy, she said the School Committee should rely on outside expertise and that writing policy without subject-matter expertise would be inappropriate.

evidence.title: Candidate profile highlights parent, advocacy, professional, and town-government experience
evidence.summary: Von Mering said she would bring perspectives as a parent of five in Winchester schools, a literacy and special education advocate, a construction and asset-planning professional, and a longtime town leader with experience in municipal finance and public process.

evidence.title: Forum coverage describes literacy and special education priorities
evidence.summary: At the School Committee forum, she supported the new literacy curriculum, said district-wide data would be unreliable for the first three years, and described advocacy around dyslexia identification, DIBELS, and early support for students.

evidence.title: Forum remarks on technology policy
evidence.summary: Discussing artificial intelligence in schools, she said the School Committee should rely on outside expertise and that she was not an expert suited to write technology policy alone.

evidence.title: 2025 Town Meeting amendment supporting school budget
evidence.summary: As a Town Meeting member, von Mering said she did not support using Circuit Breaker funds for the FY26 budget and moved an amendment to fund the school budget as requested, with additional free cash to offset the deficit.

evidence.title: Election-night summary of campaign message
evidence.summary: Election-night coverage said von Mering argued the district should close literacy and math gaps before taking on new commitments, and that both candidates called for greater attention to student mental health and said Winchester lacked a coherent technology policy.

evidence.title: Official 2026 election results
evidence.summary: Official results show von Mering received 1,861 votes for School Committee, behind John Bellaire’s 2,192.

name: John Bellaire
background: Education researcher and Winchester Public Schools graduate. Bellaire works for Bellwether, a national education nonprofit focused on state school finance, and has also worked as a youth mentor with Winchester A Better Chance and as a substitute teacher at Vinson-Owen Elementary School during the 2021-2022 school year.
summary: Bellaire presented himself as a young education-policy candidate with local school experience, emphasizing equitable and transparent school finance, earlier academic intervention, student mental health, and attention to technology in classrooms. In the 2026 election, he won the open School Committee seat.
locationNote: Winchester Public Schools graduate; attended Muraco Elementary, McCall Middle School, and Winchester High School.
priorRecordLabel: Education policy and local youth work
priorRecordSummary: His cited experience is in education research, school finance modeling, school quality reviews, mentoring Winchester ABC students, and substitute teaching, rather than prior service on the School Committee itself.
keyThemes: special education costs and early support

stanceCards.label: Transparent school finance
stanceCards.detail: Said his work includes developing financial models to help build equitable and transparent school finance systems, and he highlighted experience interrogating school budgets.

stanceCards.label: Push for faster literacy improvement
stanceCards.detail: Said Winchester has been delayed on literacy and argued for earlier support when students first fall behind.

stanceCards.label: Support student mental health
stanceCards.detail: In his candidate profile, he said his experiences motivate him to support student mental health.

stanceCards.label: Address classroom technology
stanceCards.detail: He said his experiences as a student in Winchester motivate him to address technology in the classroom.

stanceCards.label: Uses budget data in student-support arguments
stanceCards.detail: At the forum, he referenced the share of the school budget going to special education while arguing that earlier intervention could better support students.

evidence.title: Candidate profile describes education policy background
evidence.summary: Bellaire said he is an education researcher, Winchester Public Schools graduate, and nonprofit staff member who develops financial models for equitable and transparent school finance systems and conducts school quality reviews.

evidence.title: Candidate profile cites mentoring, substitute teaching, and student-focused priorities
evidence.summary: He said he works with Winchester A Better Chance, previously served as a substitute teacher at Vinson-Owen, and wants to provide high-quality student engagement opportunities, address classroom technology, and support student mental health.

evidence.title: Forum remarks on literacy delays and early intervention
evidence.summary: At the candidate forum, Bellaire said Winchester has been behind on literacy, cited worsening MCAS reading performance since 2019, and argued that addressing problems in first grade is better than waiting until students fall further behind.

evidence.title: Forum coverage describes race context and candidate background
evidence.summary: Forum coverage said Bellaire graduated from Winchester High School in 2021, studied education and public policy at Brown, and works for Bellwether, a national education nonprofit focused on state school finance.

evidence.title: 2026 election results for School Committee
evidence.summary: Official results show John H. Bellaire received 2,192 votes to Heather R. von Mering’s 1,861 in the one-seat School Committee race.

name: Keri Layton
background: Layton said she began civic work in Winchester as a Precinct 2 Town Meeting member in 2017, later served on the Master Plan Steering Committee, then served three years on the Conservation Commission before joining the Planning Board. She said she chaired the Planning Board during North Main Street rezoning and is seeking a second term.
summary: Incumbent Planning Board member with prior Town Meeting, master plan, and Conservation Commission experience. In campaign materials and the candidate forum, she emphasized balancing growth with conservation, advancing zoning changes for multifamily and accessory dwelling units, and using mixed-use development to expand the commercial tax base. She won reelection in 2026.
priorRecordLabel: Planning Board incumbent and former board chair
priorRecordSummary: Layton described serving on the Planning Board, chairing it during the North Main Street rezoning, helping advance MBTA Communities Act multifamily zoning, the Main Street Mixed Use District, and ADU by-right zoning. She also referenced participation in review of the 10 Converse Place redevelopment proposal.
keyThemes: mixed-use development and commercial tax base

stanceCards.label: Links planning to town assets including schools
stanceCards.detail: Layton said natural resources are as critical to Winchester as its historic buildings and excellent schools, framing land-use decisions as part of preserving key town assets.

stanceCards.label: Supports mixed-use growth to broaden tax base
stanceCards.detail: At the forum, Layton said mixed-use projects can expand the commercial tax base without adding students to schools.

stanceCards.label: Supports added housing and affordable units
stanceCards.detail: She said Winchester remains hundreds of subsidized units short of the 10% threshold, supported multifamily zoning near the train station, and discussed affordable housing expectations for downtown development.

stanceCards.label: Emphasizes clearer development expectations
stanceCards.detail: Layton said the board should use pre-application meetings to set affordable housing, design, and height expectations early for developers.

evidence.title: Candidate profile outlines civic and board experience
evidence.summary: Layton said she served as a Precinct 2 Town Meeting member, on the Master Plan Steering Committee, on the Conservation Commission for three years, and then on the Planning Board, where she later became chair.

evidence.title: Profile cites support for MBTA zoning, mixed-use district, and ADUs
evidence.summary: Layton said she oversaw final steps on zoning implementing the MBTA 3A Communities Act, helped bring the Main Street Mixed Use District to Town Meeting, and was proud the town passed ADUs by right.

evidence.title: Forum: Layton stresses housing need and distributed growth
evidence.summary: At the League of Women Voters forum, Layton said Winchester was still about 400 units short of the 10% subsidized housing threshold and urged creative thinking about distributing housing across town rather than concentrating density in a few corridors.

evidence.title: Forum: Layton backs clearer pre-application standards
evidence.summary: Layton said the board should tell developers early what affordable housing requirements, aesthetic expectations, and height limits apply in the downtown area.

evidence.title: Forum: mixed-use projects can broaden tax base
evidence.summary: Layton said mixed-use projects expand the commercial tax base without adding students to schools.

evidence.title: 2026 election results: Layton wins Planning Board seat
evidence.summary: Official results show Layton received 2,331 votes in the race for two Planning Board seats, finishing first and winning reelection.

name: Michelle Prior
background: Incumbent Select Board member and chair at the time of the 2026 race. Prior said she had 15 years on Town Meeting representing Precinct 4, six years on the Finance Committee including chairing FY24, three years on the Select Board with two years as chair, and service on the Audit Advisory Committee, Retiree Health Insurance Advisory Committee, and Wright Locke Farm Conservancy Board. A 2026 election-night report described her as a public-sector consultant with 35 years of experience.
summary: Prior ran for a second Select Board term emphasizing town financial planning, non-property-tax revenue, housing options for residents of different ages and incomes, and coordination across boards and committees. She won reelection in March 2026.
priorRecordLabel: Select Board chair and finance committee experience
priorRecordSummary: Sources show Prior was Select Board chair, participated in State of the Town work, and campaigned on her prior service on Town Meeting, Finance Committee, and Select Board.
keyThemes: New town revenue beyond property taxes
Housing options and aging in place

stanceCards.label: Expand non-property-tax revenue
stanceCards.detail: Called for more revenue beyond the residential property tax levy through commercial growth, fee reviews, monetizing town assets, and transfer station rate changes.

stanceCards.label: Housing for varied incomes and ages
stanceCards.detail: Said Winchester should remain a community where people of all ages and incomes can live and thrive, including housing that supports seniors aging in place.

stanceCards.label: Lean override sizing and post-defeat review
stanceCards.detail: During override debate she argued against asking voters for more than modeled needs, and after the override loss said the town should first study why it failed through listening sessions, surveys, and focus groups.

stanceCards.label: Long-term planning and coordination
stanceCards.detail: Sought to build a long-term financial plan, support the new town manager, and improve communication across boards and committees.

stanceCards.label: Override included school facilities funding
stanceCards.detail: The February 2026 override package she helped negotiate split capital funds in part toward school facilities, but the sources do not show a broader standalone facilities platform.

evidence.title: Candidate profile outlines experience and priorities
evidence.summary: Prior said she had served on Town Meeting, the Finance Committee, and the Select Board, and highlighted new revenue, housing options, communication, long-term financial planning, and renewable-energy cost savings as priorities.

evidence.title: Official minutes identify Prior as Select Board chair
evidence.summary: State of the Town meeting minutes list Michelle Prior as Select Board chair and note committee work reviewing progress of working groups.

evidence.title: Official 2026 election results
evidence.summary: Official results show Michelle I. Prior defeated Shamus M. Brady 2,849 to 1,159 for the Select Board seat.

evidence.title: Election-night coverage describes campaign emphasis
evidence.summary: Election-night coverage said Prior emphasized deliberate process and consensus-building throughout her campaign and reported that she won reelection by a wide margin.

evidence.title: Prior argued for a narrower override ask
evidence.summary: During Select Board negotiations over the Proposition 2½ override, Prior said the board should not ask voters for more money than modeling showed was necessary and raised concerns about limiting flexibility through health-insurance stabilization funding.

evidence.title: After override defeat, Prior called for public feedback first
evidence.summary: Following the ballot defeat, Prior said the town should learn why the override failed through focus groups, listening sessions, and surveys before moving ahead, while expecting another ballot question within the year.

name: Nick Rossettos
background: Incumbent Planning Board member, Winchester High School graduate, and fractional CFO for life sciences companies. He said he has spent the past four years working on mixed-use development, affordable housing approvals, MBTA 3A compliance zoning, North Main Street zoning, and design standards.
summary: Rossettos ran for reelection to the Planning Board in 2026 emphasizing experience with mixed-use and affordable housing, design standards, and preserving Winchester’s character while adding housing. He framed growth and preservation as compatible and highlighted the fiscal value of mixed-use development.
locationNote: Grew up in Winchester and graduated from Winchester High School in 1984.
priorRecordSummary: Rossettos described four years of Planning Board work, including unanimous approval of the Waterfield affordable housing mixed-use development, work on MBTA 3A and North Main Street zoning, and design standards. A forum report also identified him as an incumbent and liaison to the Design Review Committee and Historical Commission.
keyThemes: Balancing housing growth with tax-base concerns

stanceCards.label: Supports affordable and inclusionary housing
stanceCards.detail: He highlighted approval of the Waterfield affordable housing mixed-use project and said North Main Street zoning prioritizes inclusionary housing.

stanceCards.label: Focus on design and neighborhood fit
stanceCards.detail: He said experienced board members are needed so new buildings complement Winchester’s established character and stressed thoughtful design review.

stanceCards.label: Links development to tax base growth
stanceCards.detail: He said mixed-use development can increase the town’s tax base and argued the town needs a combination of development types.

stanceCards.label: Emphasizes financial discipline
stanceCards.detail: He cited his professional background as a fractional CFO and said he brings financial discipline and analytical rigor to Planning Board work.

evidence.title: Candidate profile outlines Planning Board experience
evidence.summary: Rossettos said he spent four years working on mixed-use development, including the unanimous Waterfield approval, MBTA 3A zoning, North Main Street zoning, and design standards prioritizing inclusionary housing and mixed use.

evidence.title: Forum comments on growth and preservation
evidence.summary: At a League of Women Voters forum, Rossettos said growth and preservation are not mutually exclusive and centered his campaign on preserving downtown character while adding housing.

evidence.title: Forum comments on housing pipeline and zoning
evidence.summary: He cited Waterfield, Cambridge Street, Washington and Swanton, and 10 Converse Place as examples tied to affordable or inclusionary housing efforts, and said some zoning regulations are complicated and confusing.

evidence.title: Forum comments on fiscal impacts
evidence.summary: Rossettos said developers favor residential projects because they are profitable and argued the town needs a combination, drawing on his CFO background.

evidence.title: Forum identifies incumbent role
evidence.summary: The forum coverage described Rossettos as an incumbent and liaison to the Design Review Committee and Historical Commission.

evidence.title: 2026 Planning Board election result
evidence.summary: In the March 21, 2026 town election for two Planning Board seats, Rossettos received 1,864 votes, behind Keri Layton (2,331) and Amy S. Beliveau (2,042), and was not elected.

name: Shamus Brady
background: Former Winchester School Committee member (2021–2024), former Finance Committee member during the early COVID-19 period, and a public-school administrator and educator. In 2026 he ran for Select Board while serving as director of special education for Everett Public Schools.
summary: Brady ran for Select Board in 2026 emphasizing budget expertise, support for an operating override, affordability relief, and efforts to expand commercial revenue. He presented himself as a progressive candidate with prior town finance and school governance experience. He lost the election to incumbent Michelle Prior.
locationNote: Winchester resident since 2014 and father of two children in public school.
priorRecordLabel: School Committee and Finance Committee record
priorRecordSummary: Brady cited service on the Winchester School Committee from 2021–2024 and on the Finance Committee during the early months of the COVID-19 pandemic. Meeting coverage shows him supporting added school budget flexibility for literacy-related work and asking operational questions about literacy resources.

stanceCards.label: Supports operating override
stanceCards.detail: Brady said the town needs to pass an operating override to maintain current service levels and said he would support strong stewardship of those funds.

stanceCards.label: Emphasizes school budget knowledge
stanceCards.detail: He argued his professional background and School Committee service gave him detailed knowledge of the school budget, which he described as the largest part of the town budget.

stanceCards.label: Calls for affordability measures
stanceCards.detail: He said town taxes and fees are often regressive and proposed a committee on affordability, citing the residential tax exemption as one possible relief tool.

stanceCards.label: Seeks new business revenue
stanceCards.detail: He said Winchester should more actively recruit businesses and identify what kinds of businesses would generate the most revenue without raising residential property taxes.

stanceCards.label: Public input if cuts are needed
stanceCards.detail: If an override failed, Brady said he would ask department heads to identify essential services and possible cuts, then bring proposals to the public through written feedback and forums.

evidence.title: Candidate profile outlines experience and priorities
evidence.summary: Brady described his School Committee and Finance Committee service, support for an operating override, affordability proposals, and interest in expanding business revenue.

evidence.title: Election-night coverage summarizes campaign message
evidence.summary: Coverage said Brady pushed for urgency, visible dissent, and new revenue streams, and identified him as a former School Committee member and current special education director in Everett.

evidence.title: School Committee budget discussion in 2024
evidence.summary: At a budget meeting, Brady voiced support for proposals serving students directly and said the committee needed funding flexibility for literacy curriculum work.

evidence.title: School literacy discussion in 2024
evidence.summary: During a literacy update, Brady asked whether the UFLI resource was available to all K-2 teachers across schools, showing engagement with literacy implementation details.

evidence.title: Official 2026 election results
evidence.summary: Brady received 1,159 votes in the Select Board race, losing to Michelle Prior, who received 2,849 votes.

name: Anthea Brady
background: Select Board incumbent seeking re-election in 2025; served on the board for three years, including two as vice chair. She has a Master of Public Policy and has worked in the education sector, including special education and K-12 data, and later in organizational culture and compliance work.
summary: Brady ran for another Select Board term in 2025 emphasizing continuity in town governance, support for schools, capital planning, pedestrian and playground improvements, affordable housing, and economic development. In election results, she won one of two Select Board seats.
locationNote: Lives in the Muraco neighborhood and said she had lived in Winchester for 11 years as of February 2025.
priorRecordSummary: Brady said she had served three years on the Select Board, including two as vice chair; was the board's representative to the Field Management Committee; and had previously served on the Personnel Board and as a Precinct 1 Town Meeting member. May 2025 meeting minutes list her as Select Board vice-chair.
keyThemes: affordable housing at large and small scales
commercial tax base and mixed-use development

stanceCards.label: Supports school funding
stanceCards.detail: Said the school system is a critical part of town infrastructure and should be supported with high-quality teachers, curriculum, professional development and classroom supports.

stanceCards.label: Open to override as an option
stanceCards.detail: Said the town was considering free cash or an operating override and that neither was ideal; at the 2025 candidate forum she said free cash could help in the short run but an override might be necessary.

stanceCards.label: Backs affordable housing growth
stanceCards.detail: Supported advancing both large- and small-scale affordable housing, citing projects including Waterfield, Washington and Swanton, Cross Street townhouses and possible smaller-scale housing at 160 Forest Street.

stanceCards.label: Supports expanding commercial tax base
stanceCards.detail: Pointed to MBTA station reopening, return of Laraway Road parking, North Main zoning changes and Holton Street as opportunities to expand business activity and mixed-use development.

stanceCards.label: Supports capital planning and safety projects
stanceCards.detail: Highlighted continuity around a capital plan and cited work on the library and playgrounds; also said she supported safety assessment and funding for new playgrounds at Leonard and Macdonald fields.

evidence.title: Candidate profile outlines background and priorities
evidence.summary: Brady described her education and work background, said she was seeking re-election after three years on the Select Board, and highlighted ARPA grants, traffic and pedestrian projects, playground investments, CPA work, school support, affordable housing and economic development priorities.

evidence.title: Select Board forum comments on budget and capital planning
evidence.summary: At the March 2025 candidate forum, Brady said the budget was critical because costs had risen, said free cash could help in the short run while an override might be necessary, and called for continuity around a capital plan.

evidence.title: Spoke in support of amended school budget
evidence.summary: At Town Meeting coverage in May 2025, Brady was reported speaking in support of the amended school budget and saying the schools needed help with math, the McCall schedule needed to come into compliance with the rest of the district, and the town should invest now.

evidence.title: Official minutes list Brady as vice-chair in May 2025
evidence.summary: Joint School Committee and Select Board State of the Town minutes list Anthea Brady as Select Board vice-chair.

evidence.title: Official 2025 election results
evidence.summary: Official election results show Brady received 1,649 votes in the Select Board race, finishing behind Paras Bhayani and ahead of Dorothy Simboli and Richard Welch Jr. in a two-seat contest.

evidence.title: Local news confirms Brady was re-elected
evidence.summary: Election-night coverage states that the winners in the Select Board race were Paras Bhayani and incumbent Anthea Brady.

name: Christian Nixon
background: Architect with two Rice University architecture degrees and an education policy fellowship from Northeastern University’s Institute for Educational Leadership. He said he oversees budgeting, design, and construction of capital projects for a nearby community and school district, with more than 25 years of experience in K-12 education and healthcare project delivery.
summary: Incumbent School Committee member who emphasized school finance, long-term town revenue planning, and facilities projects, including Muraco, Lincoln, and the district’s facilities master plan. He lost his 2025 reelection bid.
locationNote: Has lived in Winchester for 26 years.
priorRecordSummary: Served on Town Meeting since 2008 and on the School Committee from 2010 to 2025 election season, according to his candidate profile. He also listed service on the EFPBC, Capital Planning Committee, Audit Advisory Committee, and school facilities working groups.
keyThemes: School facilities planning and capital projects

stanceCards.label: Argued for full FY26 funding case
stanceCards.detail: Listed making the case for WPS’s 'true FY26 funding needs' as a top priority.

stanceCards.label: Supports sustainable revenue planning
stanceCards.detail: Said he wanted to support 'sustainable revenue models' to meet long-term town and district needs.

stanceCards.label: Focused on major facilities projects
stanceCards.detail: Highlighted the Muraco MSBA statement of interest, Phase II of the 10-Year Facilities Master Plan, and Lincoln Elementary improvements.

stanceCards.label: Pointed to reserve-fund budgeting tool
stanceCards.detail: Cited the Special Education Reserve Fund as a budget-management measure that helped protect educational quality amid unanticipated costs.

evidence.title: Candidate profile: professional background and local service
evidence.summary: Nixon described his architecture and education-policy background and listed service on Town Meeting, the School Committee, and multiple capital and audit bodies.

evidence.title: Candidate priorities centered on budget, revenue, and facilities
evidence.summary: He named FY26 funding needs, sustainable revenue models, the Muraco statement of interest, the facilities master plan, Lincoln improvements, and labor/culture issues as top priorities.

evidence.title: Budget answer highlighted special education reserve fund
evidence.summary: He said the School Committee approved use of more than $360,000 from the Special Education Reserve Fund to cover unbudgeted special-education costs while protecting the rest of the FY25 budget.

evidence.title: Official 2025 School Committee results
evidence.summary: In the two-seat School Committee race, Nixon received 975 votes, behind Stefanie Mnayarji and Karen Bolognese.

evidence.title: Election-night report confirmed outcome
evidence.summary: Winchester News reported that Mnayarji and Bolognese won the two School Committee seats and that incumbent Christian Nixon received 975 votes.

name: Dorothy R. Simboli
background: Lawyer with a background in commercial and residential real estate; has lived in Winchester for 25 years. Simboli has served on the Winchester Zoning Board of Appeals since 2016, including as chair since March 2024, and has been a Town Meeting member since 2016.
summary: Simboli ran for Select Board in 2025 emphasizing fiscal planning, housing and business development, and support for updated school curriculum and student services. She called for transparent budgeting, a deeper review of school spending, and said an override should be considered only if it is strategically planned and 'right sized.' She lost the two-seat race.
locationNote: Winchester resident; Town Meeting member from Precinct 6 and Zoning Board of Appeals chair.
priorRecordLabel: Town Meeting member and ZBA chair
priorRecordSummary: Her local record comes from appointed and Town Meeting roles rather than prior service on the Select Board. In Town Meeting reporting, she asked how the school literacy pilot would define and measure success after the pilot period.
keyThemes: Updated curriculum, literacy, math, and special education
Capital planning for school and town facilities

stanceCards.label: Scrutiny of school spending
stanceCards.detail: Said the school budget should get a 'deep dive' into line items, including special education costs, to evaluate how resources are used while maintaining essential services.

stanceCards.label: Supports literacy improvement with accountability
stanceCards.detail: Listed literacy among key school issues and, in Town Meeting, asked how the district would measure success for the literacy pilot.

stanceCards.label: Capital planning for school buildings
stanceCards.detail: Pointed to deferred capital needs and current and future school projects including Lynch and Muraco, calling for sound fiscal policies and prudent allocation.

stanceCards.label: Expand commercial tax base
stanceCards.detail: Advocated a more business-friendly reputation, streamlined permitting, and attention to parking to support economic development, especially along North Main Street.

stanceCards.label: Open to a 'right sized' override
stanceCards.detail: At the Select Board forum, said the town should consider an override but only with strategic planning and the right scale.

evidence.title: Candidate profile outlines background and priorities
evidence.summary: Simboli described her legal and real-estate background, ZBA and Town Meeting service, and priorities including housing, education, fiscal stability, transparent budgeting, and commercial development.

evidence.title: Candidate profile on deficit response
evidence.summary: She said Winchester's structural deficit requires detailed budget review, possible savings or revenue increases, attention to bond rating, rebidding health insurance, and a deeper review of school budget line items including SPED transportation costs.

evidence.title: Candidate profile on housing and business development
evidence.summary: She supported more collaboratively planned affordable housing, highlighted 160 Forest Street as an opportunity, and proposed streamlining permits and exploring parking solutions to strengthen the commercial base.

evidence.title: Select Board forum comments on override and planning
evidence.summary: At the League of Women Voters forum, Simboli said the town should consider an override but that it must be 'right sized' and strategically planned, and that she wanted involvement earlier in the budget process.

evidence.title: Town Meeting question on literacy pilot success measures
evidence.summary: During Town Meeting discussion of literacy funding, Simboli asked how the School Department would determine success after the pilot program was over.

evidence.title: 2025 Select Board election result
evidence.summary: Simboli received 806 votes in the four-candidate race for two Select Board seats and did not win.

name: Jack LeMenager
background: Winchester resident since 1988. Served on the Historical Commission since 2009 and has been its chair for the past eight years. Also a Town Meeting member for 15 years and a member of the executive board of the Town Meeting Members Association. Ran a write-in campaign for Planning Board.
summary: LeMenager presented himself as a candidate focused on encouraging mixed-use and commercial development while preserving Winchester’s architectural character. He emphasized better coordination among town boards and a more predictable approval process for development projects.
locationNote: His profile discusses Winchester development issues including North Main, Converse Place, the Waterfield Lot, and the town center business district.
priorRecordLabel: Historical Commission chair and Town Meeting member
priorRecordSummary: LeMenager’s prior town record is in adjacent local governance roles rather than on the Planning Board itself. He cited service on the Historical Commission, Town Meeting, and temporary committees including the train station study committee and the Community Preservation Act Advisory Committee.
keyThemes: Encourage more commercial and residential development
Support mixed-use growth in appropriate areas
Preserve architectural integrity and town character
Improve coordination across boards and commissions

stanceCards.label: No school facilities position in sources
stanceCards.detail: The provided sources do not describe positions on school facilities.

stanceCards.label: Supports more commercial development
stanceCards.detail: He argued that Winchester should encourage more commercial and residential development and specifically more commercial space in areas such as North Main.

stanceCards.label: Wants better inter-board coordination
stanceCards.detail: He said boards and commissions should communicate more and work together to encourage rational growth, with the Planning Board working more closely with the Select Board.

stanceCards.label: Backs additional housing development
stanceCards.detail: He supported greater residential and mixed-use development, including in the mixed-use district context, but the sources do not provide a detailed affordability platform.

evidence.title: Candidate profile outlines development and coordination goals
evidence.summary: LeMenager said Winchester should encourage more commercial and residential development, simplify the approval track without sacrificing core values, and improve communication among boards to support right-size mixed-use development.

evidence.title: Background in local government and preservation roles
evidence.summary: The profile says he has served on the Historical Commission since 2009, chaired it for eight years, served as a Town Meeting member for 15 years, and participated in committees including the train station study committee and CPA Advisory Committee.

evidence.title: Official 2025 election results for Planning Board
evidence.summary: Official results list Jack LeMenager with 739 write-in votes for the one Planning Board seat, ahead of Matthew Zarracina with 146.

evidence.title: Election-night report confirms Planning Board win
evidence.summary: Winchester News reported that LeMenager won the open Planning Board seat being vacated by Diab Jerius with 739 write-in votes.

name: Karen Maruyama Bolognese
background: Incumbent School Committee member and chair. Professional background includes veterinary medicine, finance, translation/interpretation, and English teaching. Education includes a DVM from Tufts, an MBA from the University of Michigan, and a BA in English from Vanderbilt.
summary: Karen Maruyama Bolognese is an incumbent School Committee chair seeking another term in 2025. In campaign materials and at a candidate forum, she emphasized long-term budgeting, district sustainability, support for the superintendent’s literacy review process, and a cautious approach to stricter device bans in schools.
locationNote: Has lived in Winchester for 14 years.
priorRecordLabel: School Committee chair and member since 2019
priorRecordSummary: Served as School Committee chair in 2021-22 and 2024-25, and as a member since 2019. Also serves as a Town Meeting member and has participated in town working groups related to facilities and housing.
keyThemes: Support for district-wide literacy review process
Broad student opportunity across academics, arts, sports, and special education

stanceCards.label: Long-term budget planning
stanceCards.detail: Advocated a long-term, planful approach to budgeting and tied it to the district’s five-year strategic planning work.

stanceCards.label: Strategic planning and prioritization
stanceCards.detail: Said she has worked with district leaders, the School Committee, and community stakeholders to prioritize long-term needs in staffing, programming, professional development, and facilities.

stanceCards.label: Acknowledges resident tax burden
stanceCards.detail: Said she takes fiscal responsibility seriously and recognizes the tax burden on residents while aiming to keep schools financially sustainable.

stanceCards.label: Supports superintendent’s literacy process
stanceCards.detail: Backed the superintendent’s approach of having educators and specialists review, pilot, and recommend literacy materials and professional development before School Committee action.

stanceCards.label: Cautious on stricter device bans
stanceCards.detail: At the forum, said phones can be distracting but argued education about wise usage and family partnership are important, and said a total ban on electronic devices would be difficult.

evidence.title: Candidate profile outlines background and current offices
evidence.summary: Profile says Bolognese has lived in Winchester for 14 years, has a professional background in veterinary medicine, finance, translation, and teaching, and has served on the School Committee since 2019, including as chair.

evidence.title: Campaign priorities stress student opportunity and fiscal sustainability
evidence.summary: She said she wants to meet diverse student needs, provide opportunities across academics, sports, special education, and arts, and keep the school budget financially sustainable over the long term.

evidence.title: Supports long-term budget planning through Winchester 2030
evidence.summary: She said she has advocated for a long-term, planful approach to budgeting and wants to collaborate on the five-year strategic plan and budget.

evidence.title: Supports district-led literacy review and pilot process
evidence.summary: She said she fully supports the superintendent’s literacy approach, with educators and specialists reviewing options, piloting them in 2025-26, and later bringing recommendations to the School Committee.

evidence.title: Forum comments on school device policy
evidence.summary: At the League of Women Voters forum, she said phones are a distraction but argued a total ban on electronic devices would be difficult and emphasized wise usage and family partnership.

evidence.title: State of the Town minutes list her as School Committee chair in May 2025
evidence.summary: Official minutes from May 30, 2025 list Karen Maruyama Bolognese as School Committee chair.

name: Paras Bhayani
background: Corporate finance and business strategy professional with prior public-sector budget experience, including leading a $1.7 billion operating and capital budget for the Chicago Transit Authority. Winchester resident for five years with involvement in local education advocacy through the Winchester Literacy Initiative for Change.
summary: Bhayani ran for Select Board emphasizing fiscal stewardship, a balanced approach to town and school funding, capital planning, housing, economic development, and sustainability. He argued Winchester would likely need additional revenue alongside efficiencies and new revenue measures, and he highlighted experience in municipal budgeting and finance.
locationNote: Has lived in Winchester for five years and said his family was drawn by the schools and community.
priorRecordSummary: No prior elected or appointed Winchester town office identified in the source packet. He cited local involvement as a founding member of the Winchester Literacy Initiative for Change and prior public-sector budget leadership outside Winchester.
keyThemes: Economic development and small business support

stanceCards.label: Balanced funding for town and schools
stanceCards.detail: Said the Select Board should manage town and school funding in a responsible, balanced manner and protect services while addressing the structural deficit.

stanceCards.label: Override likely necessary
stanceCards.detail: Said the 2019 override had already lasted longer than intended and that additional revenue would likely be needed for operating and capital needs.

stanceCards.label: Use technology for efficiency
stanceCards.detail: Proposed off-the-shelf technology to automate processes and digitize permits, approvals, and applications.

stanceCards.label: Capital planning focus
stanceCards.detail: Called for tackling a backlog of capital projects and, at the forum, referred to a five-year plan and possible override funding dedicated to capital projects.

stanceCards.label: Supports infill and affordable housing
stanceCards.detail: Backed thoughtful infill development, completion of planned projects, and redevelopment of already developed land rather than wooded open space.

evidence.title: Candidate profile outlines background and priorities
evidence.summary: Bhayani described finance and municipal budget experience, local literacy advocacy, and priorities including balanced funding, capital projects, housing, economic development, and sustainability.

evidence.title: Candidate profile says new revenue likely needed
evidence.summary: He said Winchester would need efficiencies and new revenue, and likely additional revenue beyond the 2019 override to meet town and school operating and capital needs while protecting fiscal health.

evidence.title: Candidate profile backs technology and business process improvements
evidence.summary: He proposed automation, strategic purchasing, asset utilization, and digitized approvals and permits to improve efficiency and support small business growth.

evidence.title: Forum remarks on budget shortfall and override
evidence.summary: At the March 5 candidate forum, Bhayani said Winchester faced a challenging fiscal reality, needed a comprehensive plan to balance services and costs, and after efficiencies would need to ask taxpayers for additional money.

evidence.title: Forum remarks on capital planning
evidence.summary: At the same forum, he called for a five-year plan and referred to an override dedicated for capital projects, while discussing the need to rebuild or refurbish Muraco Elementary School.

evidence.title: Official election results
evidence.summary: Official results show Bhayani received 1,945 votes in the Select Board race, the highest total in the two-seat contest.

name: Rick Welch
background: Real estate developer and home builder with 35 years of experience, including permitting and development work on multiple 40B projects statewide. He said he has spent the past decade as president of Mesiti Development Corporation, developing senior living housing.
summary: Welch ran for Select Board in 2025 emphasizing housing development experience, affordable housing, senior housing, and protecting school and sports programs while the town addresses its budget deficit.
locationNote: He said he has lived in Winchester for 35 years and that his three children graduated from Winchester Public Schools.
priorRecordSummary: Welch presented professional experience in real estate development, 40B permitting, and senior living projects as preparation for Select Board service, but the sources do not show prior elected service in Winchester.
keyThemes: Protecting school and sports spending amid deficits

stanceCards.label: Supports affordable and senior housing
stanceCards.detail: He said his development background would help Winchester address affordable housing needs and support seniors who want to remain in town, including at 160 Forest Street.

stanceCards.label: Wants to expand the tax base through development
stanceCards.detail: He called for ambitious downtown plans with commercial space below and residential above, and said 55-plus projects can bring in tax dollars.

stanceCards.label: Would avoid cuts to school and sports programs
stanceCards.detail: He said the town should find ways to address the deficit without touching school or sports programs.

stanceCards.label: No explicit override position in sources
stanceCards.detail: He discussed the structural deficit but did not state a position on an override in the provided sources.

stanceCards.label: No school facilities position stated
stanceCards.detail: The provided sources do not describe a position on school buildings or facilities.

evidence.title: Candidate profile: professional background
evidence.summary: Welch said he is a real estate developer and home builder with 35 years of experience, including multiple 40B projects and senior living development.

evidence.title: Candidate profile: reason for running
evidence.summary: He said he wants to use his development experience to help with town real estate deals, affordable housing needs, and housing for local seniors.

evidence.title: Candidate profile: structural deficit response
evidence.summary: Welch said the growing deficit is concerning and that the town should look for ways to make up for it without touching school or sports programs.

evidence.title: Candidate profile: 160 Forest Street vision
evidence.summary: He said his affordable housing experience could help navigate Winchester's housing needs and that he would like 160 Forest Street to offer opportunities for Winchester seniors struggling to stay in town.

evidence.title: Candidate profile: commercial tax base ideas
evidence.summary: Welch supported mixed-use downtown development, less parking in some projects, and incentives for 55-plus projects to grow the tax base.

evidence.title: 2025 Select Board election result
evidence.summary: In the official 2025 town election results for Select Board, Welch received 674 votes, trailing Paras Bhayani and Anthea Brady, the top two vote-getters for the two seats.

name: Stefanie Mnayarji
background: Economist with education at the London School of Economics and Northeastern University; worked in FinTech consulting for large financial institutions and later founded a Boston-based company. Mnayarji said she had lived in Winchester for eight years at the time of her 2025 profile.
summary: Mnayarji ran for School Committee in 2025 emphasizing literacy reform, broader curriculum review, intentional technology use, and school culture. She described herself as bringing elementary-school representation and prior local involvement as a Town Meeting Member and parent leader. She won one of two School Committee seats in March 2025.
locationNote: Lived in Winchester for eight years as of February 2025, according to her candidate profile.
priorRecordLabel: Town Meeting Member and parent/civic leadership
priorRecordSummary: Mnayarji said she was elected as a Precinct 5 Town Meeting Member in 2024 and had served in roles including co-chair of the Parents' Inter-School Council, TMMA board, Ambrose School Council, and Ambrose PTO president. After the election, official town minutes list her as a School Committee member and Recording Secretary at a May 30, 2025 joint meeting.

stanceCards.label: Supports literacy reform
stanceCards.detail: She identified literacy reform as a core reason for running and said curriculum updates should include classroom investments, coaching, professional development, and feedback loops.

stanceCards.label: Backs stricter device limits
stanceCards.detail: She called for intentional use of technology, review of personal device policies, and at the forum said she supported a phone-free, more broadly device-free, school experience.

stanceCards.label: Protect classroom spending
stanceCards.detail: She said budgets should prioritize classroom size, student-teacher ratios, direct student services, literacy and math curricula, teacher support, and special education.

stanceCards.label: Emphasizes transparent budgeting
stanceCards.detail: She said budget decisions should be transparent and collaborative, with town boards, teachers, parents, and community members engaged in discussion.

stanceCards.label: Links device policy to student well-being
stanceCards.detail: At the candidate forum, she said phone-free districts report better focus and improved mental health.

evidence.title: Candidate profile outlines background and motivations
evidence.summary: Mnayarji described her professional background, local civic roles, and campaign focus on funding, curriculum, literacy reform, technology policy, and school culture.

evidence.title: Candidate profile on budget priorities
evidence.summary: She said budgets should use data-driven allocation, pursue grants and partnerships, and protect classroom investments while seeking administrative efficiencies over instructional cuts.

evidence.title: Forum remarks on device-free schools
evidence.summary: At the March 6 candidate forum, Mnayarji said she supported a phone-free, broader device-free school experience and argued such policies can improve focus and mental health.

evidence.title: Official election results show School Committee win
evidence.summary: Official town results list Mnayarji with 1,964 votes in the School Committee race, ahead of Christian Nixon and alongside Karen Maruyama Bolognese for the two seats.

evidence.title: Election-night coverage confirms winners
evidence.summary: Local election coverage reported that Mnayarji and incumbent Karen Maruyama Bolognese won the two School Committee seats.

evidence.title: Post-election official minutes list her on School Committee
evidence.summary: May 30, 2025 town minutes list Stefanie Mnayarji as a School Committee member and Recording Secretary.
